result.chapter_summary: Chapter 4 argues that the failure of American education stems from teacher-training institutes which have prioritized 'romantic' ideology over empirical cognitive science. Hirsch contends these schools function more like theological seminaries than professional training centers, indoctrinating future teachers with a quasireligious 'nature knows best' philosophy that rejects systematic instruction in favor of unproven naturalistic methods.

claims.text: There is active resistance in education schools to scientific findings, particularly in the area of literacy.
claims.source_passage: It’s not just ignorance. There’s active resistance [in our education schools] to the science, too. I interviewed a professor of literacy . . . who told me she was “philosophically opposed” to phonics instruction.

claims.text: The advocacy for 'pure discovery' learning in education continues despite a lack of evidence for its effectiveness.
claims.source_passage: Like some zombie that keeps returning from its grave, pure discovery continues to have its advocates. However, anyone who takes an evidence-based approach to educational practice must ask the same question: Where is the evidence that it works?

claims.text: Teacher education is currently determined more by ideology and personal predilection than by the actual needs of children.
claims.source_passage: “The education our teachers receive today is determined more by ideology and personal predilection than the needs of our children.”

claims.text: The shortcomings of dominant educational ideas are revealed when compared against the insights of modern cognitive science.
claims.source_passage: Anyone who compares the ideas that dominate our teacher-training schools with the insights of current cognitive science must recognize their shortcomings.

claims.text: The 'nature knows best' viewpoint in education functions as a quasireligious conviction rather than a scientific one.
claims.source_passage: Yet we persist in the wrong direction, because the “nature knows best” point of view is at bottom a quasireligious conviction.

claims.text: William Heard Kilpatrick’s 1918 paper 'The Project Method' was instrumental in introducing the religious faith in nature into American schooling.
claims.source_passage: The religious faith in the rightness of nature started appearing in our schooling with the help of William Heard Kilpatrick, John Dewey’s colleague at Teachers College. His hugely influential 1918 paper “The Project Method”...

claims.text: Kilpatrick’s 'nature knows best' principle of romanticism eventually came to dominate US educational thought.
claims.source_passage: Over a relatively short time, Kilpatrick’s quasireligious “nature knows best” principle of romanticism came to dominate US educational thought.

claims.text: Explicit and systematic teaching of letter-sound correspondences is the most reliable way to enable all children to decode print accurately.
claims.source_passage: It’s an empirical question, and we have a definitive empirical answer: the fastest, most reliable way of enabling all children to decode print accurately is to teach letter-sound correspondences explicitly and systematically.

claims.text: Education schools reject explicit phonics instruction because they perceive it as artificial and 'deadly' to the child.
claims.source_passage: According to our schools of education, that “deadly, artificial” mode of instruction must be wrong and deadening because it is not a natural approach.

claims.text: The 'whole language' approach was promoted to teachers using insufficiently vetted articles in educational journals.
claims.source_passage: Insufficiently vetted articles supporting these natural methods were published in educational journals and offered to young future teachers as scientifically valid confirmations.

claims.text: The failure of students to decode print due to educational romanticism is both an educational and a moral scandal.
claims.source_passage: Because of our educational romanticism, many of our young students still struggle to decode print—a moral and an educational scandal!

claims.text: Without basic decoding ability, children cannot find pleasure in reading and will eventually experience reading as frustration.
claims.source_passage: Without that basic decoding ability, the “pleasure” of reading means nothing. Soon... reading becomes an exercise in frustration for children who never mastered phonics and can’t progress appropriately.

claims.text: Teacher-training institutions in recent decades have functioned more like theological seminaries than professional training institutes like nursing or medical schools.
claims.source_passage: In recent decades they have been more like theological seminaries than professional training institutes such as nursing or medical schools.

claims.text: Doctrines taught in many schools of education are at odds with the findings of other university departments, particularly cognitive psychology.
claims.source_passage: But doctrines in many schools of education are at odds with those of other university departments—and especially with those of cognitive psychology.

claims.text: Resistance in education schools to systematic or arduous methods stems from a belief that the mind should be built the same way as the body in nature.
claims.source_passage: The implicit reasoning goes as follows: That’s not the way nature works in building the child’s body; surely, it’s not how nature works in building the child’s mind.

claims.text: Educational schools' resistance to anything perceived as 'unnatural' results in a resistance to systematic or arduous instruction.
claims.source_passage: The ed school resistance to anything “unnatural” expresses itself as a resistance to anything that is systematic or arduous.

claims.text: Unlike other species, human infants lack survival instincts for finding food, shelter, or defense at birth.
claims.source_passage: If you leave an infant on its own, useful survival instincts do not kick in. The baby doesn’t know how to find food or shelter, how to hunt or defend against predators.

claims.text: Human survival depends on big brains and the creation of social arrangements, laws, and norms rather than biological instinct.
claims.source_passage: For humans, nature abandoned instinct and pleasure as the dominant guide because (through evolution) it found that humans survived better when their big, rational, clever brains enabled them to create powerful social arrangements that guaranteed plenty of food and drink, destroyed enemies, and created laws and social norms.

claims.text: Writing is a thoroughly cultural and non-natural phenomenon.
claims.source_passage: It is a thoroughly cultural, not natural, phenomenon, and it’s no surprise that mastery of reading should require a pragmatic, noninstinctual, artificial mode of learning.

claims.text: Mastery of reading requires a pragmatic, non-instinctual, and artificial mode of learning.
claims.source_passage: it’s no surprise that mastery of reading should require a pragmatic, noninstinctual, artificial mode of learning.

claims.text: Nature provided humans with big brains specifically to invent social arrangements that thwart and control earlier-evolved instincts.
claims.source_passage: Nature gave us big brains to invent new social arrangements that are supposed sometimes to thwart and control our earlier-evolved instincts.

claims.text: Romantic educational theorists are incorrect in assuming that nature is providential or benign at the cultural level.
claims.source_passage: Both of those fundamental errors stem from the faith that, even at the cultural level, nature is providential, that it is God’s agent and therefore benign. This is inconsistent not only with evolutionary psychology but also with the wisdom of philosophers and sages from many cultures and eras.

claims.text: Human instinct is morally unreliable and must be guided by rules and laws rather than 'what comes naturally.'
claims.source_passage: For most of recorded human history, philosophers, sages, and storytellers have insisted that human instinct is morally unreliable and needs to be guided by laws and rules, not by doing what comes naturally.

claims.text: Nature is not a socially reliable guide for human behavior, as the romantics believed.
claims.source_passage: In humans, nature is not a socially reliable guide, as the romantics believed.

claims.text: It is in the best interest of society and schools to promote altruism and rein in selfish instincts.
claims.source_passage: In other words, it is in the best interest of society and our schools to promote the altruistic thread, and to rein in our selfish selves.

claims.text: John Dewey's educational principles dominated American elementary school education after an initial period.
claims.source_passage: He articulated educational principles that, after a period, dominated America’s elementary school education.

claims.text: The psychological side of education, based on the child's instincts, is the primary basis of the educational process.
claims.source_passage: Of these two sides, the psychological is the basis. The child’s own instincts and powers furnish the material and give the starting point for all education.

claims.text: Following a child's natural instincts will automatically result in the child being properly socialized.
claims.source_passage: Dewey explained that if we conduct such child-centered education properly, following the child’s “instincts and powers,” we will automatically socialize the child.

claims.text: Providing the same curriculum to every child is unnatural because each child has a different nature.
claims.source_passage: Since each child has a different nature, to offer each child the same curriculum would be unnatural.

claims.text: Dewey proposed general critical thinking skills as a unifying principle to justify the lack of coherent, shared content in individualized education.
claims.source_passage: Dewey therefore wrote an influential book a decade later, How We Think (1910), whose basic argument was that our schools are really teaching something beyond mere content—namely, critical thinking skills.

claims.text: Psychological research has proven that there is no such thing as a reliable general expertise of 'critical thinking' or 'scientific thinking.'
claims.source_passage: As chapter 5 will show in detail, there is no reliable general expertise of “scientific thinking” or “critical thinking,” despite its being invoked continually in the mission statements of our public schools.

claims.text: The incoherent 'helter-skelter' nature of modern school content cannot be justified without the (invalid) goal of teaching general critical thinking.
claims.source_passage: Yet without identifying critical thinking as the goal, one cannot easily justify the drawbacks of incoherent helter-skelter school content.

claims.text: Persistence in educational goals unsupported by psychology or results is a moral failure.
claims.source_passage: For Americans to persist now in slogans and goals that cannot be supported either by psychology or good school results is morally blameworthy.

claims.text: Social competence, including literacy and speaking, is fundamentally dependent on shared knowledge.
claims.source_passage: No child can find individual self-fulfillment in a modern society while lacking in social competence, including effective speaking, reading, and writing, all of which are dependent on shared knowledge.

claims.text: True independence of thought and individualism are only possible after a child has achieved linguistic and cognitive mastery.
claims.source_passage: Real individualism and independence of thought comes after cognitive and linguistic mastery—not before.

claims.text: The doctrine of 'personalization' has corrupted ('vitiated') American teacher training.
claims.source_passage: Nowadays, the term used for this child-centered approach is “personalization.” In practice, that’s the main principle of educational romanticism. It’s the key doctrine that has vitiated our teacher training.

claims.text: The doctrine of personalization results in children being taught different content, particularly in language arts.
claims.source_passage: In practice, that doctrine has implied offering different content to different children, especially in the key subject of language arts.

claims.text: The term 'personalization' is the current label for the child-centered approach of educational romanticism.
claims.source_passage: Nowadays, the term used for this child-centered approach is “personalization.” In practice, that’s the main principle of educational romanticism.

claims.text: Personalizing the topics of instruction based on individual child tastes is a fundamental conceptual error.
claims.source_passage: Nonetheless, it has been a fundamental conceptual error to assume that this idea implies personalizing the topics of instruction.

claims.text: Effective personalized teaching should be understood as 'accommodation'—adjusting the delivery of a shared message to the child's capacity, rather than changing the message itself.
claims.source_passage: An excellent teacher is able to offer all children the same underlying topics and ideas, but the shared content will be accommodated to each child’s level.

claims.text: Individualism is the enemy of verbal proficiency in early language development.
claims.source_passage: In early language development, as a whole, individualism is not the friend but the enemy.

claims.text: Verbal proficiency requires the impartation of shared knowledge through a shared curriculum.
claims.source_passage: If we want to impart verbal proficiency, we need to impart shared knowledge through a shared curriculum.

claims.text: Commonality in background knowledge allows a classroom to function as a coherent society where members understand one another.
claims.source_passage: Once the social and linguistic need for commonality in background knowledge is understood, the classroom becomes a little society in which the members understand one another and make progress.

claims.text: Schools of education adopted the term 'constructivism' around 1960 to shield progressive methods from public mockery.
claims.source_passage: our schools of education adopted the term “constructivism” to continue following the same naturalistic methods introduced by progressivism, but without the drawback of the mockery to which it had been subjected.

claims.text: The educational use of the term 'constructivism' is scientifically incorrect compared to its use in mainline psychology.
claims.source_passage: “Constructivism” carries respect in cognitive psychology, but as used in education policy is incorrect.

claims.text: Scientific constructivism describes a universal mental process and does not imply any specific superior method of schooling.
claims.source_passage: Constructivism in its scientifically respectable sense makes no claims about the best ways to conduct schooling, and it carries no special implications for education.

claims.text: Whole-class instruction (the 'sage on the stage' model) is inherently constructivist because students actively shape meaning from what they hear.
claims.source_passage: In its accurate form it says that all forms of schooling are constructivist—even when students listen to lectures from a “sage on the stage.”

claims.text: When elementary school children learn multiple different topics without progression or coherence by choosing their own projects, learning can be extremely slow and disorganized.
claims.source_passage: When elementary school children learn multiple different topics without progression or coherence by choosing their own projects, topics, and subject matters, learning can be extremely slow and disorganized.

claims.text: The adoption of progressivism (under other names) as the official mode of instruction has ensured the educational decline of the United States.
claims.source_passage: The adoption of progressivism as the official mode of instruction (under a different name) has ensured the educational decline of the United States.

claims.text: The term 'constructivism' is a rhetorical trick used to preserve outmoded theories and practices of educational romanticism.
claims.source_passage: The term ‘constructivism’ is a rhetorical trick, a scientific-sounding term used to preserve the outmoded theories and practices of educational romanticism.

claims.text: Modern 'constructivist' techniques like cooperative learning reduce teacher-student engagement and force students to tutor one another without expertise.
claims.source_passage: It reduces the amount of time the teacher engages with a child and increases the time that the untutored young are tutoring each other—without a license.

claims.text: Constructivism reduces the time teachers engage with children and increases the time untutored children spend tutoring each other.
claims.source_passage: It reduces the amount of time the teacher engages with a child and increases the time that the untutored young are tutoring each other—without a license.

claims.text: Learning the skills of deciphering, enciphering, and spelling is a subject matter in its own right, distinct from literary topics.
claims.source_passage: Learning the skills of deciphering and enciphering and correctly spelling written marks is a subject matter in its own right, separate from the various literary topics of poetry and fiction.

claims.text: The 'whole language' approach mistakenly assumed alphabetic reading and writing could be assimilated to a child's natural development like speech.
claims.source_passage: ‘Whole language’ carried the naive idea that alphabetic reading and writing (which is a very late human invention) could be assimilated to a child’s natural development.

claims.text: Sentimental views of natural reading development caused a blossoming of students’ inability to read and write.
claims.source_passage: These incorrect, sentimental views caused a blossoming of students’ inability to read and write.

claims.text: Making helter-skelter, child-chosen content the substance of classroom language arts is a significant missed opportunity for education.
claims.source_passage: it is a huge missed opportunity to make helter-skelter child-chosen content the substance of language arts in the classroom.

claims.text: The Teachers College classroom library scheme has not worked well in raising reading scores.
claims.source_passage: The scheme has not worked very well in raising reading scores, but one would not know that from the Teachers College website

claims.text: The Teachers College Reading and Writing Project relies on the flawed idea that any attractive storybook will improve reading facility and background knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Further, it supports the idea that any attractive storybook will improve reading facility and add to a child’s store of background knowledge.

claims.text: Child-centered constructivist education is not optimal, particularly for students from disadvantaged circumstances.
claims.source_passage: But, as current science has shown, it is not optimal, and especially not for students from disadvantaged circumstances.

claims.text: There is no persuasive evidence that constructivist-educated adults are happier, more creative, or more fulfilled than those from coherent curricula.
claims.source_passage: there is no persuasive evidence to show that adults who are products of constructivist education are happier and more fulfilled, or more creative than those who are products of a coherent common curriculum.

claims.text: Knowledge-based schooling makes students eager and happy because ignorance is an enemy to creativity.
claims.source_passage: Knowledge-based schooling seems to make students eager and happy, for ignorance is no friend to creativity.

claims.text: The prevalence of constructivist methods in the United States explains the decline in reading scores recorded in A Nation at Risk.
claims.source_passage: the prevalence of this method in the United States helps explain the decline in reading scores recorded in A Nation at Risk.

claims.text: The shift from 'American history' to 'social studies' encouraged teachers to use student-led projects rather than direct knowledge impartation.
claims.source_passage: The former rubrics for elementary school subject matters—for instance, ‘American history’—encouraged the teacher to impart the specific knowledge directly rather than through projects whereby the student could construct learning for herself... History took on the vaguer title ‘social studies.’

claims.text: The implementation of constructivist theory in the teaching of language and literature has had a negative effect on student learning.
claims.source_passage: In sum, the effect of constructivist theory on learning language and literature has been negative

claims.text: The renaming of elementary school subjects, such as replacing 'American history' with 'social studies,' encouraged teachers to abandon direct knowledge instruction.
claims.source_passage: The former rubrics for elementary school subject matters—for instance, “American history”—encouraged the teacher to impart the specific knowledge directly rather than through projects whereby the student could construct learning for herself

claims.text: The transition to 'social studies' resulted in the abandonment of history as a serious subject in elementary school.
claims.source_passage: The name change “social studies” brought with it an abandonment of history as a serious subject in elementary school.

claims.text: In a constructivist framework, factual information is viewed as an inconvenient burden on the educational process.
claims.source_passage: Facts became inconvenient burdens. After all, it is hard not to be a “sage on the stage” if one decides to teach young children something about American history

claims.text: The neglect of coherent history instruction in early grades has led to a lack of knowledge regarding American culture, ideals, and history.
claims.source_passage: In neglecting the coherent teaching of history in the early grades, it ushered in a new ignorance of American culture, ideals, and history.

claims.text: The social studies curriculum for grades K–3 in American public education is virtually content-free.
claims.source_passage: The more closely I examined the social studies curriculum, the more my attention was drawn to the curious nature of the early grades, which is virtually content-free.

claims.text: The current K-3 curriculum excludes essential mythology, legends, biographies, and heroic tales of the nation.
claims.source_passage: It contains no mythology, legends, biographies, hero tales, or great events in the life of this nation or any other.

claims.text: The 'expanding environments' curriculum model prioritizes a child's concrete, everyday experiences over the acquisition of cultural stories and historical knowledge.
claims.source_passage: the “expanding environments” idea, which holds that we have to start not by teaching the history, songs, and stories that help define the culture, but rather that we proceed by engaging the concrete, everyday experiences of the child’s natural development.

claims.text: The 'expanding environments' approach actually hinders the acquisition of content knowledge because its sequence is trivial and repetitive.
claims.source_passage: In fact, expanding environments actually impedes content knowledge because of its trivial and repetitious sequence.

claims.text: Lessons focused on 'community helpers' (like firefighters) are superfluous because young children are already familiar with these figures.
claims.source_passage: Such lessons are superfluous (what kindergartener does not know about firefighters?) but more damagingly do not even begin to lay the groundwork for later study

claims.text: The mastery of language and history were the central objectives of American schooling from the founding of the republic through the early 20th century.
claims.source_passage: Those two fundamental aims in making citizens had been front and center in American schooling from the ratification of the Constitution through the first quarter of the twentieth century.

claims.text: Americans' factual knowledge about their nation's history, ideals, and governmental structure has declined sharply since the 1970s.
claims.source_passage: the National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP) reports that Americans’ factual knowledge about their nation’s history, its ideals, and the details of its form of government has declined sharply since the 1970s.

claims.text: Ignorance is an obstacle to creativity rather than a facilitator of it.
claims.source_passage: based schooling seems to make students eager and happy, for ignorance is no friend to creativity.

claims.text: Less than half of eighth graders know the purpose of the Bill of Rights and only ten percent understand the system of checks and balances.
claims.source_passage: Less than half of eighth graders surveyed knew the purpose of the Bill of Rights; only one in ten had age-appropriate knowledge of the system of checks and balances among our branches of government.

claims.text: The amount of time students spend learning the details of the American form of government has declined sharply since the 1970s.
claims.source_passage: tails of its form of government has declined sharply since the 1970s.

claims.text: Civic learning has been increasingly pushed aside in elementary grades where it used to be woven through the curriculum.
claims.source_passage: In recent years, civic learning has been increasingly pushed aside. In the elementary grades, civic learning used to be woven through the curriculum.

claims.text: Only slightly more than a third of current teachers report covering civic education-related subjects on a regular basis.
claims.source_passage: Today, slightly more than a third of teachers report covering civic education–related subjects on a regular basis.

claims.text: American students demonstrate a profound lack of proficiency in civics, with two-thirds scoring below 'proficient' in 2010.
claims.source_passage: Two-thirds of students scored below “proficient” on a national civics assessment administered in 2010.

claims.text: The persistent civic achievement gap between minority/low-income students and white students undermines the equality of all citizens.
claims.source_passage: This persistent civic achievement gap undermines the equality of all citizens.

claims.text: Educational reformers should rename the subject 'history and civics' and provide a grade-by-grade outline of knowledge to be learned.
claims.source_passage: To reverse this trend, an early move of any civic-minded reformer of our education should be to rename the subject “history and civics” as before, and outline the grade-by-grade knowledge of history and civics to be learned.

claims.text: An 'instructivist' approach to teaching phonics is experimentally superior to the methods currently favored in many education schools.
claims.source_passage: names numerous first-rate experimental studies showing the superiority of an “instructivist” approach to teaching phonics.

claims.text: The failure of American children to read fluently is a tragic result of education professors' resistance to promulgating current research.
claims.source_passage: She describes the resistance of US education professors to promulgate current research, with the tragic result being that a lot of American children still cannot read fluently.

claims.text: Teaching children how to decode print quickly and accurately is the most important task in modern education.
claims.source_passage: Nothing is more important in modern education than teaching children how to decode print quickly and accurately.

claims.text: Minimal guidance during instruction—including constructivist, discovery, and inquiry-based teaching—is ineffective.
claims.source_passage: “Why Minimal Guidance During Instruction Does Not Work: An Analysis of the Failure of Constructivist, Discovery, Problem-Based, Experiential, and Inquiry-Based Teaching.”

claims.text: The failure of 'whole language' reading instruction is a typical example of the general failure of constructivism as a teaching principle.
claims.source_passage: Instead of seeing the failure to teach phonics as a small failure off in the corner, it must be seen as a typical example of the general failure of constructivism as a teaching and learning principle.

claims.text: Most American teachers have been 'explicitly indoctrinated' in a false ideological 'religion' during graduate school.
claims.source_passage: for they have been explicitly indoctrinated in a false religion, and they hold on to it religiously.

claims.text: University administrators must integrate education schools with other departments to resolve the conflict between consensus science and incorrect ideological doctrines.
claims.source_passage: The administrators of our universities should start actively integrating their ed schools—and especially their ed students—into productive association with other departments on campus.

claims.text: Constructivist methods, such as discovery learning, are ineffective for African American and disadvantaged students who lack the shared knowledge of American print culture.
claims.source_passage: She pointed out that the language of the classroom itself was often meaningless to black students and to other students whose language and shared knowledge was not that of the American print culture that more advantaged American kids absorbed at home.

claims.text: The persistence of incongruence between consensus science and teacher training is an intellectual and practical disgrace.
claims.source_passage: It’s an unacceptable intellectual (and practical) disgrace that any incongruence between consensus science and teacher training should persist.

claims.text: The language of the American classroom is often meaningless to students whose background knowledge does not align with American print culture.
claims.source_passage: the language of the classroom itself was often meaningless to black students and to other students whose language and shared knowledge was not that of the American print culture that more advantaged American kids absorbed at home.

claims.text: To be a disadvantaged child is to lack the modes of speech, vocabulary, and shared knowledge of the national print culture.
claims.source_passage: To be a disadvantaged child is to lack the modes of speech, the vocabulary, and the shared knowledge of the national print culture.

claims.text: Advantaged children can succeed with child-centered discovery methods because they already possess the requisite background knowledge from home.
claims.source_passage: Kids who come from circumstances in which the language of the home is standard educated English possess both language and background knowledge that enables them to move forward in school, even when the teacher asks them to find things out for themselves.

claims.text: The essence of school disadvantage is the lack of prior knowledge needed to understand or perform specific tasks.
claims.source_passage: The essence of school disadvantage is the lack of requisite prior knowledge to understand, much less perform, a particular task.

claims.text: Constructivist teaching methods only work for children who have enough prior knowledge to deduce correct inferences.
claims.source_passage: The only way the construct-it-for-yourself mode of teaching can work for a child is if she has adequate prior background knowledge to deduce the right inferences.

claims.text: Discovery-based methods tend to increase the achievement gap between advantaged and disadvantaged children.
claims.source_passage: The discovery method will therefore tend to increase the gap between advantaged and disadvantaged children.

claims.text: Disadvantaged students gain relatively more than advantaged students when provided with explicit whole-class instruction.
claims.source_passage: But those same disadvantaged students, when given explicit whole-class, sage-on-the-stage instruction, will gain relatively more than advantaged kids will.

claims.text: Disadvantaged students gain more from explicit instruction because they are simultaneously learning both the lesson content and the necessary prior knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Here’s why: they have much more to learn. For they have to amass the needed prior knowledge as well as the knowledge focused on within the lesson itself.

claims.text: Supporters of equality should advocate for a shared-knowledge curriculum delivered through explicit instruction.
claims.source_passage: In short, anybody who favors the principle of equality should favor a definite shared-knowledge curriculum taught by the most effective means, which is almost always through compelling, explicit modes of instruction.

claims.text: There is no body of sound research supporting minimal guidance instruction for any students except the most expert.
claims.source_passage: After a half century of advocacy associated with instruction using minimal guidance instruction, it appears that there is no body of sound research that supports using the technique with anyone other than the most expert students.

claims.text: Controlled experimental studies uniformly support full and explicit instructional guidance for novice and intermediate learners.
claims.source_passage: Evidence from controlled, experimental (a.k.a. “gold standard”) studies almost uniformly supports full and explicit instructional guidance rather than partial or minimal guidance for novice to intermediate learners.

claims.text: Americans possess the inherent means and national energy to equal the scholastic performance of any other nation.
claims.source_passage: We Americans have the means and the national energy to equal the scholastic performance of any nation.

claims.text: John Dewey conceded that individualizing school paths made the tasks of teachers heavier and risked ending in student distraction.
claims.source_passage: Our teachers find their tasks made heavier in that they have come to deal with pupils individually and not merely in mass. Unless these steps in advance are to end in distraction, some clue of unity, some principle that makes for simplification, must be found.

claims.text: Human evolution favored the development of rational brains to create social arrangements that can thwart and control earlier-evolved instincts.
claims.source_passage: Nature gave us big brains to invent new social arrangements that are supposed sometimes to thwart and control our earlier-evolved instincts.

claims.text: The psychological theory of constructivism, as pioneered by Immanuel Kant, posits that the mind shapes and interprets all sensory input rather than just receiving it passively.
claims.source_passage: That view of constructivism is basically the point that Immanuel Kant made in the eighteenth century, when he showed that the mind doesn’t just receive but also shapes, or 'constructs,' what it knows.

claims.text: Children often find the 'expanding environments' curriculum to be trivial, repetitious, and deeply boring.
claims.source_passage: While this approach appears to provide an organized curricular sequence, it lacks substantial content, especially in the early elementary grades, and children tend to find its narrow focus deeply boring.

claims.text: University administrators must require all prospective teachers to complete a course in educational psychology.
claims.source_passage: One step would be to insist that all prospective teachers must take an educational psychology course.

claims.text: The Teachers College library scheme is better for children than having no reading material at all.
claims.source_passage: No one would deny that the Teachers College scheme is better than nothing at all.

claims.text: A classroom becomes a 'little society' only when its members possess the common background knowledge necessary to understand one another.
claims.source_passage: Once the social and linguistic need for commonality in background knowledge is understood, the classroom becomes a little society in which the members understand one another and make progress.

claims.text: The belief that alphabetic reading can be acquired through natural development is a naive idea that ignores the cultural, artificial nature of writing.
claims.source_passage: 'Whole language' carried the naive idea that alphabetic reading and writing (which is a very late human invention) could be assimilated to a child’s natural development.

claims.text: The evolutionary principle that altruistic groups beat selfish groups provides a scientific basis for schools to promote altruism and rein in selfishness.
claims.source_passage: 'Selfishness beats altruism within groups. Altruistic groups beat selfish groups. Everything else is commentary.' In other words, it is in the best interest of society and our schools to promote the altruistic thread, and to rein in our selfish selves.

evidence.description: Interview with a literacy professor and her colleague demonstrating philosophical opposition to phonics and scientific findings.
Richard Mayer's 2004 article in American Psychologist questioning the evidence for pure discovery learning.
evidence.source_reference: Richard E. Mayer, 'Should There Be a Three-Strikes Rule against Pure Discovery Learning?,' American Psychologist (January 2004)

evidence.description: Arthur Levine's report identifying ideology as the driver of teacher education rather than children's needs.
William Heard Kilpatrick's 1918 paper depicting schooling as artificial and proposing the 'Project Method' for growth.
A 1936 film short showing Dewey and Kilpatrick advocating for progressive educational sentiments.
evidence.source_reference: Progressive Education in the 1940s (1936 Movie Short)

evidence.description: Emily Hanford's synthesis of the definitive empirical answer regarding explicit phonics and decoding effectiveness.
evidence.source_reference: Emily Hanford, 'Why Are We Still Teaching Reading the Wrong Way?,' New York Times, October 2018

evidence.description: The biological comparison between spiders, who can weave webs at birth, and human infants, who are helpless and lack survival instincts.
Horace Mann's writing in the first issue of the Common School Journal regarding the human lack of guiding instincts and the need for direction.
Shakespeare's characterization of nature in King Lear through the character Edmund, portraying nature as the law of the strongest and most ruthless.
The evolutionary dictum that selfishness beats altruism within groups, but altruistic groups beat selfish groups.
evidence.source_reference: David Sloan Wilson and E. O. Wilson (2007)
evidence.source_passage: “Selfishness beats altruism within groups. Altruistic groups beat selfish groups. Everything else is commentary.”

evidence.description: Dewey's foundational essay outlining the psychological and sociological sides of education.
Dewey's argument that the purpose of education is to foster a 'scientific attitude of mind' rather than content mastery.
Research in psychology that contradicts the existence of transferable, general-purpose thinking skills.
evidence.source_reference: Psychology studies referenced generally (and detailed in Chapter 5)

evidence.description: The historical rebranding of 'progressivism' to 'constructivism' in educational discourse around 1960.
Cognitive psychology examples of three-dimensional construction from two-dimensional visual stimuli (one-eye viewing or paintings).
The active construction of meaning required when listening to speech on the radio.
Teacher testimony regarding the high engagement levels of well-executed whole-class sessions.
Financial and quantitative scale of the Teachers College Reading and Writing Project libraries (706 books for $4,600 in K; 689 books for $4,200 in 1st grade).
The bibliography of 154 scientific articles cited by Teachers College, which Hirsch claims do not actually prove the scheme's effectiveness in raising scores.
Reference to the decline in reading scores recorded in the landmark report 'A Nation at Risk.'
evidence.source_reference: National Commission on Excellence in Education, A Nation at Risk (1983)

evidence.description: Diane Ravitch's 1982 research into social studies curriculum, which found early grades to be 'virtually content-free.'
A 2003 report from the Thomas B. Fordham Institute describing the 'expanding environments' model as trivial and repetitive.
National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP) data showing a sharp decline in factual knowledge of history and government since the 1970s.
A report from 'The Civic Mission of the Schools' regarding the push-out of civic learning and student deficiency in knowledge of checks and balances.
evidence.source_reference: The Civic Mission of the Schools

evidence.description: 2010 national civics assessment showing two-thirds of students scored below 'proficient'.
2010 NAEP data showing black students score 24 to 30 points lower than white students in civics.
Emily Hanford's article citing experimental studies on the superiority of instructivist phonics.
evidence.source_reference: Hanford, Emily. 'Why Are We Still Teaching Reading the Wrong Way?'

evidence.description: Paul A. Kirschner's decisive article analyzing the failure of minimal guidance/constructivist instruction.
evidence.source_reference: Kirschner, Paul A. et al. 'Why Minimal Guidance During Instruction Does Not Work' (2006)

evidence.description: Lisa Delpit's analysis of how discovery learning fails students whose home culture does not match the school's 'print culture.'
Lisa Delpit's critique of constructivist methods for African American students.
The author's 1980s research comparing freshmen at the University of Virginia with students at J. Sargeant Reynolds Community College, which revealed a gap in background knowledge.
David Grissmer's study results showing explicit instruction led to a 2/3 standard deviation gain for disadvantaged students.
A meta-review of controlled experimental studies regarding minimal guidance versus explicit instruction.
evidence.source_reference: Richard E. Clark, Paul A. Kirschner, and John Sweller

concepts.author_definition: An educational approach where children learn by exploring without explicit instruction; Hirsch characterizes it as lacking evidence.
A unit of school procedure based on 'wholehearted purposeful activity in a social situation,' such as creating a school newspaper.
concepts.source_passage: The contention of this paper is that wholehearted purposeful activity in a social situation as the typical unit of school procedure is the best guarantee of the utilization of the child’s native capacities...

concepts.author_definition: A 'holistic, immersive' approach to reading that avoids explicit phonics, assuming children will naturally work out the letter-sound system through exposure to books.
concepts.source_passage: Much better to let the child work out the letter-sound system by a “holistic, immersive” way that mixes phonics “naturally and pleasurably” into a child’s exposure to books.

concepts.author_definition: The quasireligious belief that 'nature knows best' and that schooling should follow the child's 'native capacities' rather than artificial coercion.
A metaphor for teacher-training institutions that prioritize adherence to doctrine and ideology over empirical science or professional training.
The romantic belief that nature is a benign agent (effectively God's agent) that will automatically guide a child's development correctly if left unhindered.
concepts.common_alternative: Nature as an indifferent or ruthless biological reality.

concepts.author_definition: Pragmatic, non-instinctual modes of learning required for cultural achievements like reading and writing, which do not occur naturally.

The modern name for child-centered educational romanticism; specifically, the practice of choosing individualized topics based on a child's tastes, leading to different content for different children.
concepts.common_alternative: Tailoring instruction to meet individual needs without necessarily abandoning a common curriculum.
concepts.source_passage: Nowadays, the term used for this child-centered approach is “personalization.” In practice, that’s the main principle of educational romanticism.

concepts.author_definition: A purported general habit of thought or 'attitude of mind' that Dewey used as a unifying goal for education to replace specific content.
concepts.common_alternative: A set of transferable cognitive skills that can be taught independently of domain-specific knowledge.
concepts.source_passage: This book represents the conviction that the needed steadying and centralizing factor is found in adopting as the end of endeavor that attitude of mind, that habit of thought, which we call scientific.

concepts.author_definition: An innate drive Dewey believed nature provided humans to ensure a good social outcome through child-centered education.
concepts.source_passage: He said that nature had given humans a social instinct that would ensure a good social result.

concepts.author_definition: A biblical and pedagogical principle where a shared message is adjusted to the capacity of the listener without changing the underlying content.
concepts.common_alternative: Not widely used in modern secular pedagogy as a technical term for shared-content delivery.
concepts.source_passage: A much superior version of personalized teaching borrows from the biblical idea of teaching as “accommodation.”

concepts.author_definition: A rhetorical rebranding of progressivism used to justify project-based learning and the avoidance of direct teacher instruction.
concepts.common_alternative: Mainline psychological constructivism (the mental construction of meaning).
concepts.source_passage: The term “constructivism” is a rhetorical trick, a scientific-sounding term used to preserve the outmoded theories and practices of educational romanticism.

concepts.author_definition: A merged curriculum rubric that combines technical reading/writing skills with literary content, often leading to holistic 'whole language' teaching that neglects technical mastery.
concepts.common_alternative: The study of grammar, composition, and literature as a unified subject.

concepts.author_definition: A vague title for what was previously direct history instruction, favoring student-led projects about 'me, my family, my community' over historical knowledge.
concepts.common_alternative: The integrated study of the social sciences and humanities to promote civic competence.

concepts.author_definition: A curriculum sequence that organizes social studies around a slowly widening social environment (self, family, neighborhood, community, state, etc.), which Hirsch critiques as being content-poor and repetitive.
concepts.common_alternative: A developmentally appropriate pedagogical progression from the familiar to the unfamiliar.
concepts.source_passage: The basic premise is that at each grade level, each year, students are exposed to a slowly widening social environment that takes up, in turn, self/home (kindergarten), families (1st grade), neighborhoods (2nd), communities (3rd), state (4th), country (5th), and world (6th).

concepts.author_definition: A derogatory term used by constructivists to describe teachers who directly impart knowledge to students, a role the author believes is necessary for teaching history.
concepts.common_alternative: A traditional, lecture-based teacher who does not allow for student discovery.
concepts.source_passage: After all, it is hard not to be a “sage on the stage” if one decides to teach young children something about American history from colonial times to the election of Ulysses S. Grant.

concepts.author_definition: An approach to teaching that involves explicit guidance, explaining correct inferences to students, and utilizing systematic methods like phonics.
A method where students are provided necessary background knowledge up to a point and then asked to figure things out for themselves.
The specific set of shared knowledge and language norms associated with literate American society, often absorbed at home by advantaged children.
Educational practices such as discovery and inquiry learning that require students to 'work things out for themselves' rather than receiving explicit instruction.
concepts.common_alternative: A theory of learning stating that individuals construct their own understanding and knowledge of the world.
concepts.source_passage: methods that we now call constructivist: discovery learning, inquiry learning, and methods that ask children to work things out for themselves.

concepts.author_definition: A psychological distinction identifying those who lack prior knowledge (novices) and require explicit guidance, versus those who possess deep knowledge (experts).
concepts.source_passage: In the psychological literature, the distinction is drawn between “novices” and “experts.”

concepts.author_definition: A pedagogical approach where teachers provide partial guidance, expecting students to discover knowledge themselves.
concepts.source_passage: instruction using minimal guidance instruction... full and explicit instructional guidance rather than partial or minimal guidance

concepts.author_definition: The intense, faith-based adherence to the 'nature knows best' principle within education schools that persists despite contrary empirical evidence.
The shared knowledge and linguistic mastery that children must possess before they can successfully follow their own intellectual paths.
A general principle of cognitive psychology stating that the mind actively shapes perception, distinct from the educational policy that discourages direct instruction.
A classroom model characterized by mutual understanding and shared progress enabled by a common curriculum.

cases.name: American Teacher-Training Institutes
cases.description: Hirsch characterizes these institutions as the source of educational failure because they have become 'theological seminaries' that resist science and indoctrinate teachers in romantic ideology.
key_passages: In recent decades they have been more like theological seminaries than professional training institutes such as nursing or medical schools.
The ed school resistance to anything “unnatural” expresses itself as a resistance to anything that is systematic or arduous.

cases.name: American Teacher-Training Institutions
cases.description: The author characterizes these institutes as the primary source of romantic educational ideology, where doctrine is favored over cognitive science and the 'systematic' is rejected as 'unnatural.'
key_passages: In recent decades they have been more like theological seminaries than professional training institutes such as nursing or medical schools.
The ed school resistance to anything “unnatural” expresses itself as a resistance to anything that is systematic or arduous.

cases.name: Early 20th Century Progressive Schools
cases.description: Schools that first implemented child-centered personalization and were criticized by teachers and observers for having no way to track student progress due to individualized paths and lack of shared content.
key_passages: When child-centered personalization of schooling was first implemented in a few American schools in the early twentieth century, it was attacked as being too content-vague and too individualized. Critics, including many teachers, objected, 'How could anyone keep track of what the students were learning or needed to learn if each child was doing special projects and following individual paths?'

cases.name: The US Educational Decline post-1960
cases.description: Following the public mockery of 'progressivism,' schools of education adopted 'constructivism' to continue the same methods. This persistence of romantic, child-centered instruction resulted in a measurable decline in US educational performance.
key_passages: The adoption of progressivism as the official mode of instruction (under a different name) has ensured the educational decline of the United States.

cases.name: Teachers College Reading and Writing Project
cases.description: An influential project based at Columbia University that sells massive classroom libraries of 500-750 books to schools. It encourages students to choose books based on personal interest and difficulty level rather than following a coherent knowledge-based curriculum. Hirsch argues this scheme fails to significantly raise reading scores and represents a 'missed opportunity' for disadvantaged students.
key_passages: The heart of child-centered education is still Dewey’s old institution, Teachers College, Columbia University. The Teachers College Reading and Writing Project sells classroom libraries between 500 and 750 small, colorful books for each classroom at each grade level

The scheme has not worked very well in raising reading scores, but one would not know that from the Teachers College website, which promotes the classroom library scheme with an imposing bibliography of 154 scientific articles

cases.name: The Social Studies Takeover
cases.description: Beginning in the 1930s and 1940s, American elementary schools shifted from teaching coherent American history to the 'social studies' model and the 'expanding environments' curriculum. This shift replaced the goal of making citizens through shared knowledge with a focus on a child's immediate, local experiences.
key_passages: In the elementary grades, civic learning used to be woven through the curriculum. Today, slightly more than a third of teachers report covering civic education–related subjects on a regular basis.
A 2003 report from the Thomas B. Fordham Institute describes the way in which constructivist “social studies” took over our elementary schools in the 1930s and 1940s
contestable_aspects: The causal link between the curriculum change and the decline in reading scores
The characterization of social studies as 'content-free'

cases.name: The Whole-Language Fiasco
cases.description: A major educational failure where schools of education promoted 'implicit' reading pedagogy over explicit phonics, despite decisive research favoring the latter. Hirsch cites this as the most clear evidence against educational romanticism and constructivism.
key_passages: The whole-language fiasco happens to be the most decisively studied comparison of implicit versus explicit pedagogy.
Instead of seeing the failure to teach phonics as a small failure off in the corner, it must be seen as a typical example of the general failure of constructivism

cases.name: J. Sargeant Reynolds Community College study
cases.description: During the 1980s, Hirsch conducted research among students at this mostly black college. He found that students often could not answer questions because they lacked the unheard and unseen background knowledge that the questions took for granted, resulting in 'blank sheets.'
key_passages: I decided to broaden the inquiry to include students at a then mostly black college, the J. Sargeant Reynolds Community College. There I got back a lot of blank sheets. The students hadn’t a clue as to the question I was asking. The question itself had taken too much for granted.

thinkers.name: William Heard Kilpatrick
thinkers.key_idea: The Project Method and the belief that purposeful activity utilizing 'native capacities' is superior to artificial methods.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch identifies Kilpatrick as the figure who helped institutionalize the 'nature knows best' principle in US schools.
key_passages: His hugely influential 1918 paper “The Project Method” depicted ordinary schoolchildren as “dawdling” and prone to letting their minds wander.
Kilpatrick’s quasireligious “nature knows best” principle of romanticism came to dominate US educational thought.

thinkers.name: Emily Hanford
thinkers.key_idea: The scientific consensus on reading instruction (phonics) and the ideological resistance to it in education schools.
thinkers.source_work: Why Are We Still Teaching Reading the Wrong Way? (2018)
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Hanford's reporting to substantiate his claim that education schools ignore reading science.
key_passages: I beginning with this example because it offers an insight into the thought world of our teacher-training institutions.

thinkers.name: Horace Mann
thinkers.key_idea: Humans are less endowed with instinct than animals and their faculties require right direction to avoid becoming a 'living wound.'
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Mann to show that the early leaders of the common school movement understood the non-instinctual nature of human development better than modern romantics.
key_passages: The human being is less endowed with instincts for his guidance than the lower orders of animated creation.

thinkers.name: Shakespeare
thinkers.key_idea: Nature is an indifferent or evil goddess (Edmund's law), while true goodness is found in submitting nature to human moral laws.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Shakespeare to argue that historical wisdom recognized instinct as morally unreliable and in need of cultural control.
key_passages: For Edmund’s creator, Shakespeare, nature was an indifferent, sometimes evil goddess.

thinkers.name: David Sloan Wilson and E. O. Wilson
thinkers.key_idea: Evolutionary theory shows that while selfishness may benefit an individual, altruism is the key to group survival.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses their evolutionary dictum to justify why schools must interventionally promote altruism over natural selfishness.
key_passages: Evolutionary psychology has come to agree with Shakespeare. In humans, nature is not a socially reliable guide, as the romantics believed.

thinkers.name: John Dewey
thinkers.key_idea: Education should start with the child's instincts and use 'scientific thinking' as a unifying principle in place of mass-delivered content.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch views Dewey as the intellectual leader of the 'romantic' movement in American schools. He acknowledges Dewey's admirable goals but argues his psychological theories regarding nature and thinking skills have been proven wrong by science.
key_passages: Dewey offers a good introduction to the world of the American ed school, where he is held in high regard.
Dewey was right about the structure of the difficulty, but later work in psychology has demolished his proposed solution.

thinkers.name: Immanuel Kant
thinkers.key_idea: The mind does not just passively receive information but actively shapes or constructs what it knows.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Kant to define the 'accurate' or scientific version of constructivism, which he distinguishes from the 'rhetorical' version used in education schools.
key_passages: That view of constructivism is basically the point that Immanuel Kant made in the eighteenth century, when he showed that the mind doesn’t just receive but also shapes, or “constructs,” what it knows.

thinkers.name: John Milton
thinkers.key_idea: The image of 'hungry sheep' looking up and not being fed.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Milton's metaphor from 'Lycidas' to characterize the state of children in a classroom that lacks common background knowledge.
key_passages: But without such commonality, Milton’s great line in Lycidas applies: “The hungry sheep look up and are not fed.”

thinkers.name: Diane Ravitch
thinkers.key_idea: The social studies curriculum for K-3 grades is 'virtually content-free' and lacks the cultural narratives (mythology, legends, heroes) essential for education.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch quotes Ravitch at length to provide expert validation for his claim that constructivism has hollowed out the history curriculum.
key_passages: The more closely I examined the social studies curriculum, the more my attention was drawn to the curious nature of the early grades, which is virtually content-free.

thinkers.name: Arthur Levine
thinkers.key_idea: Teacher education is driven by personal ideology rather than student needs.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Levine, a former president of Teachers College, to provide 'insider' validation of his critique of education schools.
key_passages: “The education our teachers receive today is determined more by ideology and personal predilection than the needs of our children.”

thinkers.name: Paul A. Kirschner
thinkers.key_idea: Minimal guidance (constructivism, discovery learning) is an educational failure based on an analysis of cognitive psychology.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch highlights Kirschner's 2006 article as a 'decisive' piece of evidence that constructivism lacks a scientific basis.
key_passages: In 2006, the distinguished cognitive psychologist Paul A. Kirschner and two of his colleagues wrote a decisive article: “Why Minimal Guidance During Instruction Does Not Work”

thinkers.name: Lisa Delpit
thinkers.key_idea: Constructivist methods fail black students because they rely on cultural knowledge those students haven't had the chance to acquire.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Delpit to show that 'romantic' pedagogy is not only ineffective but also socially unjust and exclusionary.
key_passages: She pointed out that the language of the classroom itself was often meaningless to black students... She was particularly critical of methods that we now call constructivist

thinkers.name: Clark, Kirschner, and Sweller
thinkers.key_idea: Minimal guidance instruction is unsupported by sound research for novice learners; explicit instruction is consistently more effective in controlled studies.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites these 'distinguished cognitive scientists' to provide a scientific, 'gold standard' rebuttal to the pedagogical theories taught in ed schools.
key_passages: After a half century of advocacy associated with instruction using minimal guidance instruction, it appears that there is no body of sound research that supports using the technique with anyone other than the most expert students.

actors.name: Teachers College, Columbia University
actors.context: The institution where Dewey and Kilpatrick worked and which influenced the direction of American education.

actors.name: Arthur Levine
actors.context: Former president of Teachers College who critiqued teacher training as being driven by ideology.

actors.name: Richard E. Mayer
actors.context: Psychologist who questioned the lack of evidence for 'pure discovery' learning.

actors.name: American Education Schools
actors.context: Accused of resisting systematic instruction and clinging to a 'providential' view of nature.

actors.name: Horace Mann
actors.context: Founder of American public schools whose views on human instincts align with modern brain science.

actors.name: David Sloan Wilson
actors.context: Evolutionary biologist used to refute the romantic belief that natural instincts are socially reliable.

actors.name: American Education Schools
actors.context: The primary site where Dewey's ideas are held in high regard and where teacher training is 'vitiated' by romantic doctrines.

actors.name: Progressive Critics (Early 20th Century)
actors.context: Teachers and critics who originally questioned how individualized project-based learning could be tracked or measured.

actors.name: Graduate Schools of Education
actors.context: Identified as the source of 'vitiated' teacher training and the rhetorical misuse of 'constructivism' to hide progressive failures.

actors.name: Cognitive Psychology
actors.context: The field where 'constructivism' is a respected and accurate scientific term, contrasted with its misuse in education policy.

actors.name: Teachers College, Columbia University
actors.context: Identified by Hirsch as the 'heart' of child-centered education and the source of the Reading and Writing Project.

actors.name: National Commission on Excellence in Education
actors.context: Author of 'A Nation at Risk,' cited by Hirsch regarding the decline in reading scores.

actors.name: Thomas B. Fordham Institute
actors.context: Cited for their 2003 report criticizing the 'expanding environments' curriculum model.

actors.name: Civic Mission of the Schools
actors.context: Organization that reported on the decline of civic learning and poor assessment results among eighth graders.

actors.name: National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP)
actors.context: The primary source for data on the decline of Americans' factual knowledge of history and government.

actors.name: Civic Mission of the Schools
actors.context: An organization that provided data on the decline of civic education.

actors.name: U.S. Education Professors
actors.context: Accused of resisting research and promulgating 'false religion' and ideology over science.

actors.name: Teachers College, Columbia University
actors.context: Mentioned in relation to Arthur Levine's critique of teacher training.

actors.name: University Administrators
actors.context: The group Hirsch calls upon to reform ed schools and integrate them with other campus departments.

actors.name: Lisa Delpit
actors.context: African American educator and author of Other People's Children.

actors.name: Richard E. Clark
actors.context: Cognitive scientist documenting the failure of minimal guidance instruction.

actors.name: Paul A. Kirschner
actors.context: Cognitive scientist documenting the failure of minimal guidance instruction.

actors.name: John Sweller
actors.context: Cognitive scientist and developer of Cognitive Load Theory.

objections_raised.objection: Explicit, systematic instruction in phonics is artificial, deadly, and deadening to a child's interest.
objections_raised.response: It is an empirical fact that explicit instruction is the fastest and most reliable way to teach decoding; without this ability, children experience frustration and can never enjoy reading.

objections_raised.objection: How can anyone track student progress if every child follows an individual path with different projects?
objections_raised.source: Critics of early 20th century child-centered education
objections_raised.response: Dewey initially conceded the problem but then argued that 'critical thinking' (scientific thinking) serves as the unifying goal that justifies individual paths.

objections_raised.objection: Effective elementary teaching requires personalized instruction, which means letting children choose their own topics.
objections_raised.response: Effective personalization does not require individualizing topics; it requires 'accommodation' (adjusting the level of shared content to the child's capacity).

objections_raised.objection: Whole-class instruction ('sage on the stage') is passive and non-constructivist.
objections_raised.response: All learning is constructivist; even in a lecture, the mind is actively constructing meaning, and well-led whole-class sessions are highly engaging.

objections_raised.objection: Constructivist education leads to greater student imaginativeness, creativity, and happiness.
objections_raised.response: There is no persuasive evidence that constructivist-educated adults are happier or more creative; conversely, knowledge-based schooling makes students eager and happy because ignorance hinders creativity.

objections_raised.objection: The Teachers College library scheme is supported by 154 scientific articles.
objections_raised.source: Teachers College Reading and Writing Project website
objections_raised.response: The scheme has not worked well in practice to raise reading scores despite the bibliography, and it is not optimal compared to knowledge-based schooling.

objections_raised.objection: Discovery methods are not ineffective if they use 'scaffolding' to provide students with necessary background knowledge.
objections_raised.source: Unnamed constructivist researchers (2007 response to Kirschner)
objections_raised.response: This is essentially 'instructivist' teaching in disguise, except it wastefully waits for students to make inferences they might not reach, whereas instructivists would simply explain and move on.

objections_raised.objection: Children should be taught to 'work things out for themselves' through discovery and inquiry learning.
objections_raised.response: Children cannot work things out if they lack the background knowledge to understand the classroom language or deduce the correct inferences; this method only works for those who already know the content.

cross_chapter_refs.reference_text: As chapter 5 will show in detail, there is no reliable general expertise of “scientific thinking” or “critical thinking”
This explains the Grissmer results noted in chapter 3, in which explicit shared-knowledge instruction caused advantaged students to gain half a standard deviation...
